## Questions and Responses

### Q1: Body overlap (supervisor/SKILL.md)
Q: The supervisor/SKILL.md body has 2 sections ("Ride the Wave — Full Execution Cycle" at line 526 and "Layer Cake Parallelism" at line 874) that overlap with auto-generated figures in the header. However, each body section also contains additional prose NOT in the generated figure (exit conditions table, slice closure rules, TDD key principle note, L2 anti-pattern warning). How should we handle the overlap?
Options:
  - Remove ASCII blocks only: Delete just the fenced code blocks. Keep surrounding prose.
  - Remove entire duplicate sections: Delete the full sections (heading + prose + diagram). Risk: loses instructional prose.
  - Keep everything as-is: Accept duplication.
A: "Remove entire duplicate sections"
User note: "One thing to fix: with how the L1/L2/L3 leaf tasks are worded, models are thinking that there MUST be 3 leaf stages. This is incorrect. There can be any number of leaf tasks: L1/L2/.../L{M}."

### Q2: Sub-skill files
Q: supervisor-spawn-worker/SKILL.md and supervisor-plan-tasks/SKILL.md are entirely hand-written files (no generated header). Their diagrams use DIFFERENT formats from the auto-generated versions (numbered-step format, concrete examples). Both serve as standalone context when an agent reads only that sub-skill. Should we deduplicate these?
Options:
  - Keep sub-skill diagrams: Not true duplicates — tailored versions. No changes.
  - Remove + cross-reference: Remove ASCII blocks, add link to main supervisor SKILL.md.
  - Bring sub-skills into pipeline: Add generated headers. Scope creep: requires new RoleId entries, YAML updates, template changes.
A: "Bring sub-skills into pipeline"

### Q3: Scope timing
Q: Should bringing sub-skills into the pipeline be part of THIS followup epic or tracked as a separate epic?
Options:
  - Include in this FOLLOWUP: Expand aura-plugins-c4pa to cover both cleanup and pipeline integration.
  - Separate epic: This FOLLOWUP handles only supervisor body cleanup.
A: "Include in this FOLLOWUP"

### Q4: L{M} fix
Q: You noted models think there MUST be 3 leaf stages (L1/L2/L3). Where should this wording fix land?
Options:
  - Include in this FOLLOWUP: Fix layer cake language in modified files.
  - Separate fix: Track as own task.
  - Fix in the figure YAML: Update layer-cake.yaml itself to show L1/L2/.../L{M}.
A: "(1) and (3)" — both Include in this FOLLOWUP AND Fix in the figure YAML

### Q5: Catch-all
Q: Is there anything else from your original requirements or vision for the figures pipeline that you dont see reflected in this FOLLOWUP scope?
A: "Nothing else"
title: FOLLOWUP_URE: Deduplicate figures + bring sub-skills into pipeline

description:
The query_audit_events activity (audit_activities.py) does not forward the role parameter to AuditTrail.query_events even though the Protocol interface supports it.

## Gap
In audit_activities.py:
- Activity signature is: query_audit_events(epoch_id: str, phase: PhaseId | None = None)
- Missing: role: RoleId | None = None parameter
- The delegation call: await _AUDIT_TRAIL.query_events(epoch_id=epoch_id, phase=phase)
- Missing: role=role kwarg

The AuditTrail Protocol defines:
  async def query_events(self, *, epoch_id: str | None = None, phase: PhaseId | None = None, role: RoleId | None = None) -> list[AuditEvent]

## Fix Required
1. Add role: RoleId | None = None to query_audit_events signature
2. Forward role=role in the _AUDIT_TRAIL.query_events() call
3. Update any callers and tests accordingly

## Rationale
Without the role filter, queries that scope to a specific agent role (e.g. 'show only SUPERVISOR audit events for this epoch') silently return unfiltered results. This is a silent correctness failure.

## Acceptance Criteria
- query_audit_events activity accepts role: RoleId | None = None
- Delegation call passes role=role to _AUDIT_TRAIL.query_events()
- Test: query_audit_events with role filter returns only role-matching events
- No regressions in existing tests
title: FIX: query_audit_events drops role filter — add role: RoleId | None to activity

## Proposal Summary

Rename worker→aurad, add aura-msg CLI stub, restructure docs, improve workflow signals and test infrastructure.

## Vertical Slice Decomposition

### SLICE-1: Rename + Packaging (R1, R3, R4)
Mechanical rename of worker→aurad across 19 locations + persistent SQLite + packages.default.

**Files owned:**
- bin/worker.py → bin/aurad.py (rename + update internal references)
- flake.nix (packages.worker → packages.aurad, add to default)
- nix/temporal-service.nix (dbPath default + XDG resolution + mkdir -p)
- tests/test_worker.py → tests/test_aurad.py (rename + update WORKER_PATH→AURAD_PATH)
- CHANGELOG.md (update reference)

**Does NOT touch:** RoleId.WORKER, protocol.yaml owner_roles, types.py enums

### SLICE-2: aurad systemd + aura-msg stub (R2, R5)
New systemd service for aurad + aura-msg CLI stub.

**Files owned:**
- nix/aurad-service.nix (NEW — systemd user service for bin/aurad.py)
- bin/aura-msg.py (NEW — stub with argparse --help, planned subcommands)
- flake.nix (packages.aura-msg, homeManagerModules.aurad-service, update default)
- tests/test_aura_msg.py (NEW — test stub --help, parse_args)

### SLICE-3: Doc restructure (R6)
Split docs/worker.md into 3 files + add roadmap.

**Files owned:**
- docs/worker.md → DELETE (content migrated)
- docs/architecture.md (NEW — overall design, Temporal topology, aurad/aura-msg model)
- docs/aurad.md (NEW — daemon ops: systemd, config, running locally)
- docs/aura-msg.md (NEW — CLI stub docs, planned commands, hook integration vision)
- Roadmap section in docs/architecture.md with full R12 stub descriptions

### SLICE-4: Workflow signals + ReviewAxis type fix (R7, R8)
Signal-based slice progress + ReviewAxis/VoteType in ReviewPhaseWorkflow.

**Files owned:**
- scripts/aura_protocol/workflow.py:
  - SliceInput: add parent_workflow_id field
  - New SliceProgressSignal dataclass
  - EpochWorkflow: new @workflow.signal slice_progress handler + @workflow.query slice_progress_state
  - SliceWorkflow: signal parent with per-leaf-task completion
  - ReviewPhaseWorkflow._votes: dict[str,str] → dict[ReviewAxis,VoteType]
  - ReviewPhaseResult.vote_result: dict[str,str] → dict[str,str] (kept as str for serialization boundary — but internal state uses enums)
- tests/test_workflow.py (update child workflow tests)

### SLICE-5: Constraint violation fixtures (R9)
Add constraint_violations axis to protocol.yaml.

**Files owned:**
- tests/fixtures/protocol.yaml (add constraint_violations axis — all 23 C-*)
- tests/fixtures/fixture_loader.py (add ConstraintViolationTestCase, generate_constraint_violation_test_cases())
- tests/test_protocol_combinatorial.py (add parametrized constraint violation tests)
- tests/test_fixture_loader.py (add tests for new generator)

## Integration Points

| Integration Point | Owner | Consumers | Contract | Timing |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| packages.aurad name | SLICE-1 | SLICE-2 (aurad-service.nix needs package ref) | packages.aurad in flake.nix | SLICE-1 before SLICE-2 |
| bin/aurad.py path | SLICE-1 | SLICE-2 (systemd ExecStart), SLICE-3 (docs) | bin/aurad.py exists | SLICE-1 before SLICE-2, SLICE-3 |
| SliceInput.parent_workflow_id | SLICE-4 | N/A (self-contained) | New field on SliceInput | Independent |
| ConstraintViolationTestCase | SLICE-5 | N/A (self-contained) | New dataclass in fixture_loader.py | Independent |

## Dependency Order
```
SLICE-1 (rename) → must complete first (other slices reference aurad paths)
SLICE-2 (systemd + stub) → depends on SLICE-1 (references packages.aurad)
SLICE-3 (docs) → depends on SLICE-1 (references bin/aurad.py)
SLICE-4 (signals + type fix) → independent (workflow.py only)
SLICE-5 (constraint fixtures) → independent (test files only)
```

Parallel execution: SLICE-1 first, then SLICE-2+3+4+5 in parallel.

## Tradeoffs

| Decision | Rationale |
|---|---|
| Separate aurad-service.nix (not extending temporal-service.nix) | Decoupled lifecycle — can restart aurad without restarting Temporal server |
| ReviewPhaseResult.vote_result stays dict[str,str] | Serialization boundary — StrEnum is safe for dict keys but vote_result crosses the workflow→caller boundary |
| All 23 constraints in YAML with skip markers | Documentation value + future-proofing. Skip markers clearly communicate what's testable today |
| aura-msg stub with --help only | Defer full implementation to a focused epoch. Stub establishes the binary, package, and planned API surface |

## Acceptance Criteria
- Given aurad binary, when `bin/aurad.py --help`, then shows usage with namespace/task-queue/server-address options
- Given aura-msg stub, when `bin/aura-msg.py --help`, then shows planned subcommands (start-epoch, signal-vote, query-state, advance-phase)
- Given temporal-service enabled, when service starts, then auto-creates SQLite parent directory and uses XDG_DATA_HOME path
- Given SliceWorkflow runs, when leaf task completes, then parent EpochWorkflow receives progress signal
- Given constraint violation fixtures, when pytest runs, then 5 implemented constraints produce runnable tests and 18 skip
- All existing 1196 tests continue to pass
title: PROPOSAL-1: aurad rename + aura-msg stub + protocol improvements

description:
tests/test_worker.py uses unittest.mock (mock.patch, mock.patch.dict) for testing bin/worker.py CLI args and env vars. This is the FIRST mock usage in a codebase with 799+ baseline tests that achieve zero-mock testing through DI and structural subtyping.

Files: tests/test_worker.py (lines 1-327)
Imports: from unittest import mock (line ~8)
Usage: mock.patch.dict('os.environ', ...) for env var tests, mock.patch for sys.argv

Impact: Sets precedent that future tests can use mocks, weakening the codebase's strong DI-first testing convention. Worker CLI arg parsing could potentially be tested via DI (inject argv list) instead of mocking sys.argv.

Recommendation: Refactor to pass argv as parameter to parse_args() and use os.environ.copy() with dict merge instead of mock.patch.dict. This aligns with the existing pattern where all other test files use constructor injection.
title: IMPORTANT: test_worker.py introduces unittest.mock — breaks codebase no-mock convention

description:
---
references:
  request: aura-plugins-dqfz
---
## Questions and Responses

### Round 1: Highest-Leverage Boundaries

#### Q1: Figure Placement
Q: Should figures render as part of the existing Workflows section (inline after each workflow), or as a separate Figures section in the generated SKILL.md?
Options:
  - Inline with workflow: Each figure renders immediately after its associated workflow's structured stages/actions. The ASCII diagram complements the structured data. Requires figure→workflow association via workflow_ref field.
  - Separate Figures section: A standalone '### Figures' section in the generated output, after Workflows. Figures are independent of workflows and can illustrate constraints, phases, or anything. More flexible but less contextual.
  - Both (dual rendering): Workflow-associated figures render inline after the workflow. Figures with section_ref != 'workflows' get their own section. Most flexible but more template complexity.
A: Inline with workflow

#### Q2: MVP Figure Scope
Q: For MVP scope, which figures should be created? The 2 existing YAML files (layer-cake for worker, ride-the-wave for supervisor) are ready. Should we also create new figures for other roles?
Options:
  - Just the 2 existing: Integrate only layer-cake.yaml and ride-the-wave.yaml. Prove the pipeline works with what we have. New figures can be added later.
  - Existing + architect state flow: The 3 roles with workflows (worker, supervisor, architect) each get a figure. Create architect-state-flow.yaml to match the existing Architect State Flow workflow.
  - All roles with workflows + new candidates: Create figures for all 3 workflow roles plus new figures for reviewer (severity tree), epoch (12-phase diagram). Broader coverage but more content authoring.
A: Existing + architect state flow

### Round 2: Dependent Decisions

#### Q3: Data Model
Q: The existing YAML figures use section_ref: workflows as a generic pointer. For inline rendering, we need a specific workflow_ref field (e.g., workflow_ref: ride-the-wave). Should we replace section_ref with workflow_ref, or keep both?
Options:
  - Replace section_ref with workflow_ref (Recommended): Simpler model. Since figures are inline with workflows, section_ref is redundant. Each figure points to exactly one workflow via workflow_ref. If a figure doesn't have a workflow, it's not rendered (future extension).
  - Keep both fields: section_ref stays for future non-workflow figures (e.g., standalone constraint diagrams). workflow_ref added as optional for inline placement. More flexible but more fields to validate.
A: Keep both fields

#### Q4: Deduplication
Q: The supervisor/SKILL.md has a hand-written copy of the Ride the Wave diagram (lines ~495-521) below the END marker. Once the figure renders inline in the generated section, should we remove the hand-written copy to deduplicate?
Options:
  - Yes, remove hand-written copy (Recommended): The generated figure replaces the hand-written diagram. No duplication. This is the whole point of schema-driven generation.
  - Keep both temporarily: Leave the hand-written copy during MVP. Remove it in a follow-up pass after verifying the generated output is correct.
A: Keep both temporarily

### Round 3: Edge Cases

#### Q5: Source of Truth
Q: Should figures be loaded from YAML files at import time (like a YAML-driven dict), or should figure content be defined inline in types.py (like WORKFLOW_SPECS currently is)?
Options:
  - Inline in types.py (Recommended): Consistent with WORKFLOW_SPECS, CHECKLIST_SPECS, CONSTRAINT_SPECS — all defined as Python literals in types.py. The YAML files become secondary/derived. Single source of truth stays in Python.
  - Load from YAML files: YAML files in skills/protocol/figures/ are the source of truth. types.py has a loader that reads them at import time. YAML is easier to author/edit but adds runtime file I/O and a second format to maintain.
  - YAML as source, generate types.py entry: YAML files are authoritative. A build step converts them to Python dict entries in types.py. Best of both worlds but adds a build step.
A: (Custom) Have a types.py but should not be a string containing the entire figure: just a name for it. Then load from YAML for figure content. Hybrid model — types.py defines Figure metadata (id, title, type, role_ref, section_ref, workflow_ref) without embedding the content string. YAML files are the source of truth for the actual ASCII content. Content is loaded at generation time.

#### Q6: Catch-All
Q: Anything else about figures that the above questions didn't cover?
Options:
  - Nothing else: Everything is covered. Proceed to URD and proposal.
  - I have additional context: Let me add something the questions didn't capture.
A: Nothing else
title: ELICIT: ASCII diagram figures for skill generation pipeline

## Scope
Define frozen dataclasses and lazy _registry_path() function in bin/aura-release.

## Deliverables
- _registry_path() -> Path: lazy function (NOT module-level constant)
- PluginEntry(name: str, path: Path, remote: str | None) frozen dataclass
- MarketplaceEntry(path: Path, plugins: tuple[PluginEntry, ...]) frozen dataclass
- PluginRegistry(marketplaces: tuple[MarketplaceEntry, ...]) frozen dataclass with method stubs

## File
- bin/aura-release (add after imports section)
title: SLICE-3-L1: Types — PluginEntry, MarketplaceEntry, PluginRegistry, _registry_path()

references:
  request: aura-plugins-t8r0
  elicit: aura-plugins-sgcl

## Requirements

### R1: Code Examples (HAS-A on Constraints and Procedure Steps)
Add `examples: tuple[CodeExample, ...]` to ConstraintSpec and ProcedureStep.
- Schema: `<example>` child elements under `<constraint>` and `<step>` with CDATA content
- Attributes: id (required), lang (closed enum: bash/go/python/pseudo/xml/json/markdown), label (closed enum: correct/anti-pattern/context/template), also-illustrates (optional cross-ref)
- All examples inline regardless of size — no external file references
- BCNF: example is FD on its parent constraint/step; cross-refs via also-illustrates attribute

### R2: Role-Specific Prose
Two new child elements on `<role>`:
- `<introduction>`: 1-2 sentence role description rendered as opening paragraph
- `<ownership-narrative>`: Conceptual "What You Own" text explaining the role's domain ownership
- Both are 1:1 with role — inline child elements, not separate sections

### R3: Role Behaviors
New `<behaviors>` child element on `<role>` for tactical GWT guidance that is role-scoped but NOT protocol constraints.
- Format: same GWT/S structure as constraints but semantically distinct (tactical guidance vs protocol rules)
- Rendered in a separate "Role Behaviors" section in generated output, distinct from "Constraints"
- Examples: worker's "plan backwards from production code path", supervisor's "use model: haiku for trivial changes"

### R4: Completion Checklists
Separate top-level `<checklists>` section with role-ref foreign keys.
- `<checklist>` keyed by (role-ref, gate) where gate is closed enum: completion, slice-closure, review-ready, landing
- `<item>` children with id, required boolean, and text content
- BCNF: sibling section with foreign keys, no role element bloat

### R5: Inter-Agent Coordination Commands
Separate top-level `<coordination-commands>` section.
- `<coord-cmd>` elements with id, action (human-readable), template (bd command)
- Shared commands: shared="true", no role-ref (rendered for all roles)
- Role-specific commands: role-ref foreign key
- BCNF: shared commands stored exactly once

### R6: Workflow Diagrams
Separate top-level `<workflows>` section. ALL three workflows schematized:
- Ride the Wave (supervisor): multi-stage with conditional-loop review cycles, exit conditions
- Layer Cake (supervisor/worker): TDD parallelism pattern
- Architect State Flow: linear state progression
- `<workflow>` with id, name, role-ref, description
- `<stage>` children with id, name, phase-ref, order, execution mode (sequential/parallel/conditional-loop)
- `<action>` children within stages
- `<exit-condition>` for conditional-loop stages with type (success/continue/escalate/proceed)

### R7: Review Axes Rendering
Render review axes table in generated section from existing schema.xml `<review-axes>/<axis>/<key-questions>`.
- Adds "### Review Axes" table to generated header for reviewer role
- Deduplicates hand-written review axes content

### R8: Generator Pipeline Updates
- types.py: New enums (ExampleLabel, ExampleLang, GateType, WorkflowExecution) + new dataclasses (CodeExample, ChecklistItem, Checklist, CoordinationCommand, WorkflowAction, ExitCondition, WorkflowStage, Workflow, BehaviorSpec)
- schema_parser.py: Parse new child elements and new top-level sections
- gen_skills.py: Build new context entries for template rendering
- skill_header.j2: New template sections for all new schema elements
- context_injection.py: Extend RoleContext with new fields
- gen_schema.py: Emit new XML sections from Python types

### R9: Deduplication
Manual review per SKILL.md file after generation. Remove:
- Duplicate `# {Role} Agent` headings below END marker
- "12-Phase Context" sections (covered by Owned Phases table)
- "Audit Trail Principle" sections (covered by C-audit-never-delete constraint)
- GWT items that are now formal constraints or behaviors
- Skills tables (covered by Commands table)
- Review axes tables (now generated)
- Inter-Agent Coordination tables (now generated)
- Completion checklists (now generated)

### R10: Test Updates
- test_schema_parser.py: Count tests for new entities, structure tests for new child elements
- test_schema_types_sync.py: Sync tests for new Python types against schema.xml
- test_gen_skills.py: Template rendering tests for new sections across all 4 roles

## Priorities
1. Schema structure (R1-R7) — defines the data model
2. Generator pipeline (R8) — makes it work
3. Deduplication (R9) — cleans up the output
4. Tests (R10) — ensures correctness

## Design Choices
- Separate top-level sections for checklists, coordination, workflows (BCNF purity)
- Inline children for 1:1 relationships (introduction, ownership-narrative on <role>)
- New <behaviors> element for role-scoped tactical GWT guidance (distinct from protocol constraints)
- All code examples inline with CDATA (no external file references)
- All 3 workflows formally schematized
- Review axes rendered in generated section
- Manual deduplication after generation

## MVP Goals
All 6 extension categories (R1-R7) + generator pipeline (R8) + dedup (R9) + tests (R10) in one epoch. No phasing — single complete migration.

## End-Vision Goals
Every piece of structured, role-scoped content in SKILL.md files is programmatically generated from schema.xml. The hand-written body contains only unique tactical guidance that cannot be expressed as structured data (e.g., detailed narrative explanations, conceptual frameworks).
title: URD: Schema extensions to fully generate SKILL.md

description:
---
references:
  request: aura-plugins-cuuw
  urd: aura-plugins-s7l0
  supersedes: aura-plugins-po9n
---
## Changes vs PROPOSAL-1

Addresses Reviewer B findings (aura-plugins-oitf):
1. Corrected import strategy — `spec_from_file_location` returns None for extensionless files
2. Explicit ordering: dead code removal is a hard prerequisite for the test
3. Two additional test cases (Case C: root + subdir, Case D: invalid subdir pyproject.toml)
4. Expanded deletion range to include orphan comment at line 32

---

## Problem Space

**Root cause (recap):** Installed nix binary (495 lines) reads `VERSION_FILES['pyproject']` unconditionally at module level; crashes when git root has no `pyproject.toml`. Develop branch already fixes core logic via `discover_version_files()`. Two cleanup tasks remain.

---

## Changes

### Change 1: Remove dead code from `bin/aura-release` (lines 32–57)

Delete lines 32–57 inclusive. Line 32 is an orphaned comment (misplaced above the dead block; its correct counterpart is at line 58 above `COMMIT_SECTIONS`):

```
32: # Conventional commit prefix -> Keep a Changelog section  ← orphan; delete
33: def _discover_repo_root() -> Path: ...
48: REPO_ROOT = _discover_repo_root()
50: VERSION_FILES = {'pyproject': ..., 'plugin': ..., 'marketplace': ...}
56: CHANGELOG_PATH = REPO_ROOT / 'CHANGELOG.md'
57: (blank line)
```

After deletion, line 58 (`# Conventional commit prefix → ...`) follows directly after line 31's blank line and correctly introduces `COMMIT_SECTIONS`.

**Impact:** purely subtractive. Removes one git subprocess at every invocation.

### Change 2: Add regression test `tests/test_aura_release.py`

**Ordering note (hard prerequisite):** Change 2 MUST be written and run after Change 1 is applied. If lines 33–57 still exist, importing the module executes `REPO_ROOT = _discover_repo_root()` at load time, which calls `sys.exit(2)` inside any plain `tmp_path` (not a git repo), making every test crash with `SystemExit(2)` before assertions run.

**Import strategy:** `bin/aura-release` has no `.py` extension. `importlib.util.spec_from_file_location(name, path)` returns `None` for extensionless files (Python determines the loader from the file extension). The correct approach uses an explicit `SourceFileLoader`:

```python
from importlib.machinery import SourceFileLoader
import importlib.util

BIN_PATH = Path(__file__).resolve().parent.parent / 'bin'

def _import_aura_release():
    loader = SourceFileLoader('aura_release', str(BIN_PATH / 'aura-release'))
    spec = importlib.util.spec_from_loader('aura_release', loader)
    mod = importlib.util.module_from_spec(spec)
    spec.loader.exec_module(mod)
    return mod
```

No `pythonpath` change in `pyproject.toml` is needed — `SourceFileLoader` takes an absolute path directly.

**Test cases (class `TestDiscoverVersionFiles`):**

**Case A: pyproject.toml at repo root (existing happy path)**
```
tmp/
  pyproject.toml   ← [project]\nversion = '1.0.0'
```
Assert: returns exactly one `PyprojectVersionFile` with `path == tmp/pyproject.toml`.

**Case B: pyproject.toml only in subdirectory (the original bug scenario)**
```
tmp/
  opencode-security-filter/
    pyproject.toml  ← [project]\nversion = '0.1.0'
```
Assert: returns exactly one `PyprojectVersionFile` with `path == tmp/opencode-security-filter/pyproject.toml`.

**Case C: both root and subdirectory have pyproject.toml**
```
tmp/
  pyproject.toml             ← [project]\nversion = '2.0.0'
  sub/
    pyproject.toml           ← [project]\nversion = '1.0.0'
```
Assert: returns exactly two entries; root entry is first (scan order preserved).
Guards against a future short-circuit where only the first match is returned.

**Case D: subdirectory pyproject.toml exists but has no [project] version key**
```
tmp/
  tool-only/
    pyproject.toml  ← [tool.foo]\nbar = 'baz'  (no [project] section)
```
Assert: returns empty list.
Guards against `_has_pyproject_version` filter being accidentally removed.

**No git repo needed:** `discover_version_files` uses only `pathlib` operations. `pytest`'s `tmp_path` fixture is sufficient.

---

## Engineering Tradeoffs

| Option | Pros | Cons | Decision |
|--------|------|------|----------|
| SourceFileLoader (explicit) | Correct for extensionless files | More verbose than spec_from_file_location | **Do it** |
| spec_from_file_location | Simpler | Returns None for extensionless files — broken | Rejected |
| subprocess test (CLI) | Tests full binary | Requires git repo setup, slower | Deferred |
| 4 test cases (A–D) | Closes real regression gaps | Slightly more than minimum | **Do it** (low cost, high value) |

---

## MVP Milestone

1. Delete lines 32–57 from `bin/aura-release` (verify: no references to dead symbols remain)
2. Create `tests/test_aura_release.py` with `TestDiscoverVersionFiles` (Cases A–D)
3. Run `pytest tests/test_aura_release.py` — all pass
4. Update nix profile (`nix profile upgrade` or rebuild home-manager) to ship new binary

---

## Public Interfaces (unchanged)

`discover_version_files(root: Path) -> list[VersionFile]` — existing API, no changes.

---

## Validation Checklist

- [ ] Lines 32–57 deleted from bin/aura-release
- [ ] No references to _discover_repo_root / REPO_ROOT / VERSION_FILES / CHANGELOG_PATH remain (grep confirms)
- [ ] tests/test_aura_release.py created with TestDiscoverVersionFiles
- [ ] Case A passes: root-level pyproject.toml discovered
- [ ] Case B passes: subdirectory-only pyproject.toml discovered (original bug scenario)
- [ ] Case C passes: both root and subdirectory returned, root first
- [ ] Case D passes: subdir without [project] version → empty list
- [ ] pytest runs clean (no new failures)

---

## BDD Acceptance Criteria

**Given** a git repo with no pyproject.toml at root **when** a subdirectory contains a valid [project] pyproject.toml **then** discover_version_files returns that file **should not** raise FileNotFoundError

**Given** dead code removed **when** aura-release imported via SourceFileLoader **then** no git subprocess spawned at import time **should not** call _discover_repo_root at module level

**Given** Case C structure **when** both root and sub have valid pyproject.toml **then** both are returned with root first **should not** short-circuit after first match

**Given** Case D structure **when** sub pyproject.toml has no [project] version **then** discover_version_files returns empty list **should not** crash or return file without version
design: {"validation_checklist":["Lines 32-57 deleted; grep confirms no dangling references","tests/test_aura_release.py created","Case A passes","Case B passes (original bug)","Case C passes (root+sub)","Case D passes (no [project] version)","pytest clean"],"acceptance_criteria":[{"given":"repo with no root pyproject.toml","when":"subdirectory has valid pyproject.toml","then":"discover_version_files finds it","should_not":"raise FileNotFoundError"},{"given":"dead code removed","when":"imported via SourceFileLoader","then":"no git subprocess at import","should_not":"call _discover_repo_root"},{"given":"root and sub both have valid pyproject","when":"discover_version_files runs","then":"returns both, root first"},{"given":"sub pyproject has no [project] version","when":"discover_version_files runs","then":"empty list returned"}],"tradeoffs":[{"decision":"SourceFileLoader","rationale":"spec_from_file_location returns None for extensionless files"},{"decision":"4 test cases","rationale":"Cases C and D close real regression gaps at near-zero extra cost"},{"decision":"delete line 32 too","rationale":"orphaned comment would duplicate line 58 after deletion"}]}
title: PROPOSAL-2: aura-release reads pyproject.toml from wrong directory

The proposal's AC10 validation checklist item states: 'End-to-end pipeline test: parse_schema → gen_types (draft) → gen_schema (with diff) → validate_schema → gen_skills (with diff) pipeline succeeds.'

No such test exists in any of the 4 slice test files. The components are tested individually:
- test_schema_parser.py: parse_schema tested in isolation
- test_gen_schema.py: round-trip uses parse_schema as part of AC3 (covers parse + gen_schema + validate)
- test_gen_skills.py: generate_skill tested independently
- test_gen_types.py: generate_types_source tested independently

The full chain (parse → gen_types → gen_schema → validate → gen_skills) is never exercised in a single test. AC10 explicitly requires this chain to succeed as a unit.

Remediation: Add a test (possibly in test_gen_schema.py or a new test_pipeline.py) that exercises the full chain: calls parse_schema on real schema.xml, generates a draft via generate_types_source, then calls generate_schema, validates the output, and finally calls generate_skill on a marked SKILL.md. This is an integration test that confirms all modules compose correctly.

description:
VOTE: REVISE

## Elegance Review: PROPOSAL-1 (aura-plugins-vouf)

### Summary

The proposal is well-structured and closely follows established codebase patterns. However, it introduces two design choices that are disproportionate to the problem size and inconsistent with how the rest of the pipeline handles the relationship between content and its container.

### Finding 1 (REVISE): FigureType enum is premature — single-member enum adds abstraction without value

**Current state:** FigureType has exactly 1 member: `ASCII_DIAGRAM = 'ascii-diagram'`.

**Problem:** A single-member enum provides zero discriminating power. It exists only to hold the string `'ascii-diagram'`. Every code path will match on this one value. The enum adds a new import, a new type, and a new schema element (`<enum name="FigureType">`) — all to carry a constant string. None of the existing enums in types.py were introduced with only one member; they all discriminate between at least 2 values from day one (VoteType has ACCEPT/REVISE, Domain has user/plan/impl, etc.).

**Recommendation:** Use a plain string literal `'ascii-diagram'` on the Figure dataclass's `type` field for MVP. When a second figure type is introduced (e.g., `mermaid`, `svg`), THEN promote to an enum. This follows YAGNI proportionally — the enum is the right abstraction at 2+ members, but dead weight at 1.

**Severity:** Not a blocker on its own, but it contributes to the overall feeling that the proposal is slightly over-specified for what is essentially "load 3 YAML files and render them inline."

### Finding 2 (REVISE): figure_by_workflow dict in gen_skills.py is a separate lookup structure when the data already flows through workflows

**Current state:** The proposal creates a `figure_by_workflow: dict[str, Figure]` keyed by `workflow_ref`, built in gen_skills.py and passed alongside the `workflows` list to the template.

**Problem:** This is a parallel data structure for something that has a natural home. Every figure in the MVP has exactly one `workflow_ref`. The template already iterates `{% for wf in workflows %}` and then separately looks up `figure_by_workflow[wf.id]`. This means the template needs to know about two separate data sources for what is conceptually "stuff that belongs to a workflow."

**Alternative considered:** Adding a `figure: Figure | None` field to the Workflow dataclass itself. This would mean:
- No separate dict to construct and pass
- Template simply checks `{% if wf.figure %}`
- The HAS-A relationship (Workflow HAS-A Figure) is expressed in the type system, not in a side-channel dict
- context_injection already filters workflows by role_ref — figures would flow naturally

**Why this matters for elegance:** The existing pipeline pattern is: dataclass in types.py → filtered by role in context_injection.py → passed to template → rendered. The Workflow already follows this pattern. Adding a figure field to Workflow means figures follow the exact same path with zero new plumbing. The `figure_by_workflow` dict is a second parallel path for the same data.

**Counterargument addressed:** The proposal mentions `section_ref` for "future non-workflow figures." If that future arrives, a separate `section_figures` list can be introduced then. For MVP where all 3 figures are workflow-associated, the natural home is on the Workflow.

**Severity:** This is the primary REVISE finding. The current design works but creates unnecessary indirection that will confuse future readers who expect figures to "just be part of the workflow."

### Finding 3 (ACCEPT with note): section_ref + workflow_ref redundancy

Both `section_ref: str` and `workflow_ref: str | None` exist on Figure. For MVP, section_ref is always `'workflows'` and workflow_ref is always non-None. This is technically redundant — if you know the workflow_ref, you know the section is 'workflows'. The user explicitly chose to keep both for future extensibility, which I respect as a deliberate tradeoff. However, if Finding 2 is adopted (figures live on Workflow), section_ref becomes even more redundant since the association to the workflows section is implicit.

### Finding 4 (ACCEPT): YAML loading approach fits naturally

The hybrid model (metadata in types.py, content in YAML) parallels how the existing pipeline separates schema structure from rendered content. The YAML files are clean, the loading point in context_injection is the right place (it already does role-filtering), and the error handling approach (actionable errors on missing files) matches the existing SchemaParseError pattern.

### Finding 5 (ACCEPT): Template rendering approach is appropriate

The `##### heading + fenced text block` rendering is proportional and matches how other sections use heading levels (#### for workflow name, ##### for sub-elements). The fenced code block preserves ASCII art formatting correctly.

### Finding 6 (ACCEPT): Schema XML emission matches existing patterns

The `_build_figures()` function follows the identical pattern of every other `_build_*()` function in gen_schema.py: create parent element, iterate specs, add child elements with attributes. This is clean and consistent.

### Verdict

VOTE: **REVISE**

The primary issue is Finding 2: the `figure_by_workflow` dict is an unnecessary parallel data structure when figures have a natural home on the Workflow dataclass. This isn't about blocking functionality — it's about keeping the pipeline's data flow clean and proportional. The single-member FigureType enum (Finding 1) is a minor secondary concern.

**Minimum change for ACCEPT:** Either (a) move figures onto the Workflow dataclass and eliminate the separate dict, OR (b) provide a clear rationale for why the separate dict is preferable even in the MVP, given that all 3 figures are 1:1 with workflows.
title: PROPOSAL-1-REVIEW-C-1: Figures pipeline elegance

description:
---
references:
  request: aura-plugins-cuuw
  urd: aura-plugins-s7l0
---
## Problem Space

**Axes:** single-process, single-file, internal-only change

**Root cause:** `bin/aura-release` (installed nix binary, 495 lines) defines at module-load time:
```python
REPO_ROOT = _discover_repo_root()          # git subprocess at import
VERSION_FILES = {'pyproject': REPO_ROOT / 'pyproject.toml', ...}
```
Then `read_pyproject_version()` reads `VERSION_FILES['pyproject']` unconditionally.
When the git repo root has no `pyproject.toml` (e.g. `agentfilter/` repo where the
package lives in `agentfilter/opencode-security-filter/`), this crashes with
`FileNotFoundError`.

**Current develop branch (637 lines)** already replaces this with
`discover_version_files(root)` which checks `.is_file()` before reading and also
scans one level deep (`*/pyproject.toml`). The core crash is fixed. Two cleanup
tasks remain.

## Changes

### Change 1: Remove dead code from `bin/aura-release`

Delete lines 33–57 (inclusive):
```python
# DELETE — never referenced after the discover_version_files refactor:
def _discover_repo_root() -> Path: ...   # lines 33-45
REPO_ROOT = _discover_repo_root()        # line 48 — git subprocess at import\!
VERSION_FILES = {                         # lines 50-54 — never read
    'pyproject': REPO_ROOT / 'pyproject.toml',
    'plugin': ...,
    'marketplace': ...,
}
CHANGELOG_PATH = REPO_ROOT / 'CHANGELOG.md'  # line 56 — never read
```

Impact: purely subtractive. No behaviour changes. Removes one spurious git
subprocess at every invocation.

### Change 2: Add regression test `tests/test_aura_release.py`

Cover the subdirectory case so it cannot regress. Test class:
`TestDiscoverVersionFiles` — two cases:

**Case A: pyproject.toml at repo root (existing happy path)**
```
tmp/
  pyproject.toml   ← [project] version = '1.0.0'
```
Assert `discover_version_files(tmp)` returns exactly one `PyprojectVersionFile`
with path `tmp/pyproject.toml`.

**Case B: pyproject.toml only in subdirectory (the bug scenario)**
```
tmp/
  opencode-security-filter/
    pyproject.toml  ← [project] version = '0.1.0'
```
Assert `discover_version_files(tmp)` returns exactly one `PyprojectVersionFile`
with path `tmp/opencode-security-filter/pyproject.toml` (one-level-deep discovery).

**Import strategy:** `aura-release` has a hyphenated name so use
`importlib.util.spec_from_file_location` to import it as a module. Because
`repo_root()` has `@functools.lru_cache` but is lazy (not called at import time
after dead code removal), the import is safe with no side effects.

**No git repo needed** for the unit tests — `discover_version_files` takes a
`Path` directly and uses only `pathlib` operations (no git calls).

## Engineering Tradeoffs

| Option | Pros | Cons | Decision |
|--------|------|------|----------|
| Remove dead code | Cleaner import, no wasted subprocess | Minor diff noise | **Do it** |
| Leave dead code | Zero risk | Wastes subprocess, misleads readers | Rejected |
| Subprocess test (CLI) | Tests full binary | Requires git setup, slower | Deferred |
| Unit test (importlib) | Fast, no git needed, precise | Hyphen import slightly awkward | **Do it** |

## MVP Milestone

1. Delete lines 33–57 from `bin/aura-release`
2. Create `tests/test_aura_release.py` with `TestDiscoverVersionFiles` (Cases A + B)
3. Run `pytest tests/test_aura_release.py` — both pass
4. Update nix profile (`nix profile upgrade` or rebuild home-manager) to ship new binary

## Public Interfaces (unchanged)

`discover_version_files(root: Path) -> list[VersionFile]` — already exists,
this proposal only adds tests for it. No API changes.

## Validation Checklist

- [ ] Lines 33–57 deleted from bin/aura-release; no references to _discover_repo_root / REPO_ROOT / VERSION_FILES / CHANGELOG_PATH remain
- [ ] tests/test_aura_release.py created with TestDiscoverVersionFiles
- [ ] Case A passes: root-level pyproject.toml discovered
- [ ] Case B passes: subdirectory pyproject.toml discovered (regression guard)
- [ ] pytest runs clean (no new failures)
- [ ] aura-release --check runs without FileNotFoundError in a repo with only a subdirectory pyproject.toml

## BDD Acceptance Criteria

**Given** a git repo with no pyproject.toml at root **when** a subdirectory contains a valid pyproject.toml **then** discover_version_files returns that file **should not** raise FileNotFoundError

**Given** dead code is removed **when** aura-release is imported **then** no git subprocess is spawned at import time **should not** call _discover_repo_root at module level

**Given** regression test exists **when** discover_version_files changes in future **then** subdirectory case is covered **should not** allow silent regression
design: {"validation_checklist":["Lines 33-57 deleted; no dangling references","tests/test_aura_release.py created","Case A (root pyproject.toml) passes","Case B (subdirectory pyproject.toml) passes","pytest clean","aura-release --check works in subdirectory repo"],"acceptance_criteria":[{"given":"git repo with no pyproject.toml at root","when":"subdirectory contains valid pyproject.toml","then":"discover_version_files returns that file","should_not":"raise FileNotFoundError"},{"given":"dead code removed","when":"aura-release imported","then":"no git subprocess at import","should_not":"call _discover_repo_root at module level"}],"tradeoffs":[{"decision":"unit test via importlib","rationale":"hyphenated filename; lazy repo_root() means import is safe after dead code removal"},{"decision":"remove CHANGELOG_PATH too","rationale":"it depends on REPO_ROOT and is never read; part of the same dead-code block"}]}
title: PROPOSAL-1: aura-release reads pyproject.toml from wrong directory

description:
---
references:
  slice: aura-plugins-2j4v
  reviewer: cartographer-c
  round: 1
---
## Issue

Both `gen_schema.py` and `context_injection.py` define module-private dicts named `_ROLE_CONSTRAINTS` and `_PHASE_CONSTRAINTS`, but with completely different types and inverted semantics:

- `context_injection._ROLE_CONSTRAINTS: dict[RoleId, frozenset[str]]` — maps role → set of constraint IDs
- `gen_schema._ROLE_CONSTRAINTS: dict[str, str | None]` — maps constraint_id → comma-separated role-ref string (for XML attribute)

The `_CI_` import alias in gen_schema.py mitigates confusion locally, but downstream consumers (test_gen_schema.py) import `_ROLE_CONSTRAINTS` from gen_schema without context about the name collision.

## Concrete Fix (deferred)

Rename the gen_schema versions to `_CONSTRAINT_TO_ROLE_REF` and `_CONSTRAINT_TO_PHASE_REF` to make the inversion explicit. This would clarify that they map constraint→attribute-value rather than role→constraints.

## Severity Rationale

MINOR: The existing `_CI_` prefix aliasing and clear section headers prevent actual bugs. This is a naming clarity improvement, not a correctness issue.
title: MINOR: _ROLE_CONSTRAINTS/_PHASE_CONSTRAINTS name shadowing between gen_schema.py and context_injection.py

description:
---
references:
  request: aura-plugins-18zp
  urd: aura-plugins-o7i9
  elicit: aura-plugins-iv5j
  proposal: aura-plugins-fl5p
  round1_review: aura-plugins-m0gg
---

## VOTE: ACCEPT

### Axis: Elegance (C) — Complexity matching and API cleanliness, Round 2

### Summary

PROPOSAL-2 addresses both of my Round 1 structural concerns. The revision produces a cleaner, more uniform design than PROPOSAL-1. Each concern is evaluated below.

---

### Round 1 Concern 1: _coerce_violation_args removed — RESOLVED

**What was wrong:** PROPOSAL-1 introduced a stringly-typed _coerce_violation_args(method_name, raw_args) function that branched on method_name strings to decide how to coerce YAML values. This coupled the fixture loader to every check_*() method signature and would break silently on signature changes.

**How PROPOSAL-2 resolves it:** The _coerce_violation_args function is completely absent. The proposal specifies a single, minimal coercion: if "phase" is a string in violation_args, coerce it to PhaseId. This is the one coercion YAML cannot handle natively (enums are not representable in YAML). Booleans, ints, strings, and lists all pass through from yaml.safe_load() without transformation.

**Assessment:** This is the minimum necessary coercion. Only C-supervisor-cartographers requires it (phase: "p8" must become PhaseId.P8_IMPL_PLAN). All other violation_args values are native YAML types. The single-line coercion is proportional to the problem. Resolved cleanly.

---

### Round 1 Concern 2: _factory pattern removed — RESOLVED

**What was wrong:** PROPOSAL-1 introduced a _build_audit_violation_state(factory_name) function with a magic _factory key inside violation_args, creating a second dispatch mechanism that made the YAML non-uniform.

**How PROPOSAL-2 resolves it:** The two audit constraints (C-audit-never-delete, C-audit-dep-chain) now use the existing violation_state YAML path with an optional transition_history list extension. The fixture loader constructs TransitionRecord objects from these YAML entries, just as it already constructs review_votes dicts. The YAML is self-documenting:

- C-audit-never-delete: violation_state with current_phase: p2 and no transition_history (empty list default triggers the violation)
- C-audit-dep-chain: violation_state with current_phase: p2 and a transition_history entry where triggered_by is empty string

**Assessment:** This is the correct approach. The transition_history extension is a natural generalization of the existing violation_state builder — adding one more field that EpochState already has (transition_history: list[TransitionRecord] at state_machine.py line 52). No new dispatch mechanisms, no magic keys. State-based constraints use violation_state; method-based constraints use violation_method+violation_args. The boundary is crisp and uniform. Resolved cleanly.

---

### Verification Item 3: Three dispatch paths are well-separated

The test dispatch logic has three clean, mutually exclusive branches:

1. **State path** (tc.violation_state is not None): Routes to check_state(), which internally aggregates check_audit_trail() and other state-based checks. Used by the 4 existing state-based constraints plus the 2 audit constraints.
2. **Method path** (tc.violation_method is not None): Routes via getattr(_CHECKER, tc.violation_method)(**tc.violation_args). Used by 19 constraints (15 structural + 4 naming).
3. **Transition path** (fallback with assert): Routes to check_handoff_required(). Used by 1 constraint (C-handoff-skill-invocation).

The three paths correspond to three genuinely different check semantics (state inspection, method invocation, transition validation). No path overlaps with another. The ConstraintViolationTestCase dataclass enforces mutual exclusion via its nullable fields: exactly one of (violation_state, violation_method, violation_from_phase) is non-None for runnable cases.

**Note on check_structural:** My Round 1 review suggested routing the 15 structural constraints through check_structural(**kwargs) instead of individual check_*() methods. PROPOSAL-2 chose to dispatch to individual methods directly via getattr. I accept this choice for two reasons: (a) check_structural is an aggregator that runs ALL applicable checks based on which kwargs are non-None — passing a single constraint's args might inadvertently trigger overlapping checks if parameter names collide (e.g., slice_id is used by both check_blocker_dual_parent and check_slice_has_leaf_tasks), and (b) the individual methods are the true units of behavior, and testing them directly is more precise. The getattr dispatch is clean and the method names come from trusted YAML, not user input.

---

### Verification Item 4: Module organization

The changes touch exactly three test files and one fixture file:
- tests/fixtures/protocol.yaml — YAML data extension (transition_history, violation_method/violation_args entries)
- tests/fixtures/fixture_loader.py — Two additions: (1) ConstraintViolationTestCase gets two new fields, (2) generate_constraint_violation_test_cases() gets a new violation_method branch and transition_history construction
- tests/test_protocol_combinatorial.py — Three-way dispatch, partition update, docstring update
- tests/test_workflow.py — start_time_skipping() to start_local() swap

No production code changes. The fixture_loader extensions are additive (new dataclass fields default to None; new generator branch is a continue-based insertion before the existing state path). The module boundaries are clean: data definition (YAML) is separate from data loading (fixture_loader.py) is separate from test logic (test files).

---

### Verification Item 5: Single PhaseId coercion is the minimum needed

Confirmed by examining all 21 violation_args entries in the proposal:
- Only C-supervisor-cartographers has a PhaseId-typed parameter (phase: "p8").
- All other parameters are str, bool, int, or list[str] — all natively representable in YAML.
- The coercion is a single conditional: if "phase" in raw_args and isinstance(raw_args["phase"], str): raw_args["phase"] = PhaseId(raw_args["phase"]).
- This is the irreducible minimum. There is no way to avoid it because YAML has no enum type.

---

### End-User Alignment Assessment

1. **Who are the end-users?** Contributors running and extending the test suite.
2. **What would they want?** Two uniform patterns: state-based constraints use violation_state; method-based constraints use violation_method+violation_args. No hidden dispatch mechanisms.
3. **How would this affect them?** The YAML is self-documenting. Adding a new constraint test means either adding a violation_state block or a violation_method+violation_args block. One coercion rule to know about (phase strings become PhaseId).
4. **Implementation gaps?** None found. The three dispatch paths cover all 26 constraints.
5. **MVP scope?** Correct: 24 un-skipped, 1 documented skip (C-actionable-errors), 4 Temporal sandbox.
6. **Validation checklist?** Complete and correct, covering both positive (tests pass) and negative (no regressions, no removed patterns) assertions.

### Verdict

**ACCEPT** — Both Round 1 structural concerns are resolved cleanly. The _coerce_violation_args and _factory patterns are gone, replaced by a single PhaseId coercion and a natural transition_history extension to violation_state. The three dispatch paths are well-separated with clear mutual exclusion. The overall design complexity is proportional to the innate complexity of the problem (three different kinds of constraint checks require three dispatch branches — no more, no less).
title: PROPOSAL-2-REVIEW-C-2: Un-skip 25 tests — Elegance axis

description:
VOTE: ACCEPT — Reviewer A (Correctness axis)

## Per-Requirement Assessment

### R1: Figure Dataclass in types.py — PASS
The proposal defines a `Figure` frozen dataclass with fields: `id`, `title`, `type` (FigureType enum), `role_ref` (RoleId), `section_ref` (str), `workflow_ref` (str | None), `content` (str, default empty). Content is NOT embedded — it lives in YAML files. The dataclass pattern faithfully matches existing patterns (Workflow, WorkflowStage, WorkflowAction, etc. are all frozen dataclasses). The placement after WorkflowExecution for FigureType and after Workflow for Figure follows the codebase's existing organization.

### R2: FIGURE_SPECS Dict in types.py — PASS
The proposal defines FIGURE_SPECS as `dict[str, Figure]` keyed by figure id, placed after WORKFLOW_SPECS. This parallels the WORKFLOW_SPECS pattern exactly. All 3 entries have `content=''` (metadata only). The role_ref values (WORKER, SUPERVISOR, ARCHITECT) correspond to valid RoleId enum members confirmed in the codebase.

### R3: YAML Content Loading — PASS
The proposal assigns content loading to context_injection.py via `_load_figure_content(figure_id: str) -> str`. This reads from `skills/protocol/figures/{figure_id}.yaml` and populates the `content` field at generation time. This is consistent with the pipeline: types.py is metadata source of truth, YAML is content source of truth. The function raises actionable errors if the file is missing or malformed — consistent with the error handling patterns in schema_parser.py.

### R4: Inline Workflow Rendering — PASS
The proposal creates a `figure_by_workflow` lookup dict in gen_skills.py (keyed by `workflow_ref`), passes it to the Jinja2 template, and the template renders figures inline after each workflow's exit conditions as a fenced code block (```text ... ```). The template placement (inside the workflow loop, after exit conditions) is correct — confirmed by reading skill_header.j2 lines 134-154. The heading uses `##### {{ fig.title }}` (h5, one level below the h4 workflow heading) which is appropriate hierarchy.

### R5: Keep Both section_ref and workflow_ref — PASS
Both fields are present on the Figure dataclass. `section_ref` is the generic pointer (e.g., 'workflows'), `workflow_ref` is the specific workflow association. This supports future non-workflow figures as the URD requires.

### R6: Three Figures for MVP — PASS
1. layer-cake.yaml (exists, needs `workflow_ref: layer-cake` added)
2. ride-the-wave.yaml (exists, needs `workflow_ref: ride-the-wave` added)
3. architect-state-flow.yaml (NEW, full content provided in proposal)

Confirmed: both existing YAML files currently lack a `workflow_ref` field. The proposal correctly identifies this gap and specifies updating both files. The new architect-state-flow figure content covers Phases 1-7 which aligns with the architect role's owned phases. The workflow_ref values ('layer-cake', 'ride-the-wave', 'architect-state-flow') all correspond to valid keys in the existing WORKFLOW_SPECS dict.

### R7: Schema XML Generation — PASS
The proposal adds `_build_figures(root)` to gen_schema.py, called after `_build_workflows()`. The function creates `<figures>` with child `<figure>` elements containing metadata attributes (id, title, type, role-ref, section-ref, workflow-ref). Content is NOT emitted in schema.xml — consistent with the hybrid source of truth decision. The attribute naming uses kebab-case (`role-ref`, `section-ref`, `workflow-ref`) matching the existing XML convention for reference attributes.

### R8: Schema XML Parsing — PASS
The proposal adds `figures: dict[str, Figure]` to SchemaSpec and a `_parse_figures(root, path)` function parallel to `_parse_workflows`. This follows the exact pattern of other parse functions. The proposal correctly calls `_parse_figures` in `parse_schema()` and includes the result in the SchemaSpec constructor.

### R9: No Hand-Written Dedup (MVP) — PASS
The proposal explicitly states hand-written diagrams remain untouched below the END marker. The validation checklist includes "Hand-written not removed" as a verification item. The BDD acceptance criteria also specify "hand-written copies remain below END marker untouched" and "should not deduplicate during MVP".

### R10: Context Injection Extension — PASS
The proposal extends RoleContext with a `figures: tuple[Figure, ...]` field. In `get_role_context()`, it filters FIGURE_SPECS by `role_ref`, loads content from YAML, and returns Figure instances with populated content. This follows the existing pattern for workflows (lines 466-470 of context_injection.py), checklists (lines 452-456), and coordination_commands (lines 459-463).

## Internal Consistency Check

### Types and Fields — CONSISTENT
- FigureType enum uses StrEnum (matches all other enums in types.py)
- FigureType.ASCII_DIAGRAM = 'ascii-diagram' (matches the `type: ascii-diagram` in existing YAML files)
- Figure dataclass fields map cleanly to YAML fields and XML attributes
- role_ref uses RoleId (not raw strings) — strongly typed, consistent with project constraints

### Pipeline Flow — CONSISTENT
types.py (metadata) -> YAML (content) -> context_injection.py (merge) -> gen_skills.py (template context) -> skill_header.j2 (render) -> SKILL.md (output)
types.py (metadata) -> gen_schema.py (XML emit) -> schema.xml -> schema_parser.py (XML parse) -> SchemaSpec

### Dependency Graph — CORRECT
types.py is the root dependency. YAML updates, gen_schema, schema_parser, and context_injection can proceed in parallel after types.py. gen_skills + template depend on context_injection. Tests depend on everything. This mirrors how other entities (Workflow, Checklist, CoordinationCommand) were integrated.

## Minor Observations (non-blocking)

1. The proposal uses `**{'role-ref': fig.role_ref.value}` dict unpacking in gen_schema.py to handle the hyphenated XML attribute. This is a valid Python pattern, consistent with how `_build_workflows` handles `role-ref` on line 1485.

2. The `content: str = ''` default on Figure allows FIGURE_SPECS entries to be constructed without content, which is then populated at load time. This is clean and avoids None-checking.

3. The proposal does not specify what happens if a FIGURE_SPECS entry has a `workflow_ref` that does not exist in WORKFLOW_SPECS. The test plan mentions "workflow_ref valid" sync tests, which would catch this at test time. Acceptable for MVP.

## Verdict

All 10 URD requirements (R1-R10) are faithfully addressed. The proposal is internally consistent: types, field names, pipeline flow, and dependency ordering all align with each other and with the existing codebase patterns. No logical inconsistencies found. No requirements missed.
title: PROPOSAL-1-REVIEW-A-1: Figures pipeline correctness

Aggregated IMPORTANT and MINOR findings from Phase 10 code review of Protocol Engine v2.

## IMPORTANT Findings

### B-SLICE3: StrictUndefined regression test isolation (aura-plugins-c5nx)
Test creates its own jinja2.Environment instead of testing gen_skills.py's actual Environment. If someone removes StrictUndefined from gen_skills.py, the test still passes. Fix: mock or patch the Environment construction inside gen_skills.py, or restructure test to call generate_skill() and verify UndefinedError is raised on an undefined variable.

### B-SLICE4: EPOCH Ride the Wave constraints lack direct inclusion tests (aura-plugins-rthn)
C-supervisor-cartographers, C-integration-points, C-slice-review-before-close, C-max-review-cycles are verified indirectly via test_gen_schema.py role-ref assertions, but test_context_injection.py has no positive inclusion tests for EPOCH role specifically containing these 4 constraints. Fix: add parametrized tests for each of the 4 constraints in EPOCH's frozenset.

## MINOR Findings

### C-SLICE2: Name shadowing between gen_schema.py and context_injection.py (aura-plugins-prbl)
Both modules define _ROLE_CONSTRAINTS and _PHASE_CONSTRAINTS but with different types and inverted semantics. gen_schema.py's versions map roles→sets-of-constraint-IDs (for XML emission), while context_injection.py's are the authoritative hand-authored versions. Suggested rename in gen_schema.py: _CONSTRAINT_TO_ROLE_REF and _CONSTRAINT_TO_PHASE_REF to make the direction explicit.

### C-SLICE3: Lazy import in _constraints_for_role() (aura-plugins-jwoo)
Import inside the function body when no circular import risk exists. Move to module-level for clarity.
title: FOLLOWUP: Non-blocking improvements from Protocol Engine v2 code review

description: The ratified proposal (Module 1) specifies SchemaSpec.constraints as dict[str, ConstraintSpec], matching the existing ConstraintSpec dataclass. The implementation uses dict[str, tuple[str, str, str, str]] instead. The SchemaSpec docstring documents this as intentional, but this creates two divergent representations of the same constraint data: ConstraintSpec in types.py and tuples in SchemaSpec. Any consumer of SchemaSpec.constraints cannot use ConstraintSpec methods/fields directly and must destructure tuples. This is a correctness concern: the implementation diverges from the proposal's stated type contract without a documented rationale in the proposal or code beyond the inline docstring comment. Impact: any downstream code (e.g., future consumers of SchemaSpec) must know to expect tuples, not ConstraintSpec objects. The round-trip test (test_roundtrip_constraint_fields_match) destructures tuples correctly but the inconsistency is a maintainability risk.
title: SLICE-1: SchemaSpec.constraints uses tuple not ConstraintSpec — proposal/docstring mismatch

description:
## Finding

### Location
`scripts/aura_protocol/constraints.py`, `check_blocker_gate()` method (lines 353-377).

### Description
The method `check_blocker_gate` returns a `ConstraintViolation` with `constraint_id='C-worker-gates'`. However, `C-worker-gates` in CONSTRAINT_SPECS is defined as:

> given: worker finishes implementation, when: signaling completion, then: run quality gates (typecheck + tests) AND verify production code path

This constraint is about worker completion gates (typecheck + tests + no TODOs), NOT about the p10→p11 BLOCKER resolution gate. The BLOCKER gate is actually part of `C-review-consensus` or could be argued to belong to a dedicated `C-blocker-gate` constraint.

Meanwhile, the dedicated method `check_worker_gates(has_todos, tests_pass, typecheck_pass)` ALSO uses `C-worker-gates` for a different purpose (worker quality gates).

This means a consumer calling `check_all(state)` at p10 with blockers will receive a `C-worker-gates` violation that conflates two different enforcement concerns under the same constraint ID.

### Severity
MINOR — no functional bug. Tests correctly verify the violation is raised. The constraint_id mismatch is a naming/categorization issue.

### Suggestion
Introduce a dedicated `C-blocker-resolution-gate` constraint in CONSTRAINT_SPECS (and schema.xml) for the p10→p11 gate, or reuse `C-review-consensus` (which already handles p10→p11) for the blocker count check. Update check_blocker_gate to use the appropriate constraint_id.
title: SLICE-3-REVIEW-A-1 MINOR: check_blocker_gate references C-worker-gates but semantically belongs to blocker resolution

description:
VOTE: REVISE - Three correctness gaps found:

## BLOCKER-1: Double-bump prevention not enforced by code

SLICE-5 states the marketplace will NOT be double-bumped if already in _SCAN_SPECS, but the proposed pseudocode in SLICE-5 has no actual path-comparison guard against the already-discovered `files` list. The integration code only checks `mp_path.is_file()`. If a user registers their local `.claude-plugin/marketplace.json` path in the registry (which is the expected canonical use case per ELICIT), it WILL be bumped twice — once by discover_version_files() and once by the registry integration path. A concrete guard is required: e.g., `if mp_path not in {vf.path for vf in files}:` before calling `mp.write()`.

## BLOCKER-2: Marketplace path missing from rollback_paths

The existing rollback boundary (lines 522-574 of bin/aura-release) builds `rollback_paths` from discovered `files` + CHANGELOG.md on line 524, before the try block. SLICE-5 inserts the marketplace write inside that try block but the marketplace path is never added to `rollback_paths`. If the marketplace.json write succeeds but a later step (git commit, tag) fails, rollback() will NOT restore marketplace.json, leaving it at the bumped version while the rest of the working tree is rolled back. The proposal must either: (a) add mp_path to rollback_paths before the try, or (b) make the registry write the very last step so it does not need rollback coverage.

## BLOCKER-3: Registry load() deserialization does not validate entry structure

The proposed validation rules handle: invalid JSON (exit 2), missing `marketplaces` key (exit 2), and nonexistent marketplace paths (warn + continue). But the transition from raw JSON dicts to frozen dataclasses (PluginEntry, MarketplaceEntry) is not validated. If a registry entry is missing required keys (`name`, `path`), the dataclass constructor raises a bare TypeError with no user-friendly message. The proposal must specify what happens when individual plugin entries are malformed: error + exit(2) per entry, or warn + skip? Leaving this unspecified means the implementation will crash with an unhelpful traceback, violating the spirit of R6 ('error clearly on parse failure').

## Non-blocking observation: find_plugin() resolve semantics unspecified

The auto-detect logic compares 'path in registry entry' to cwd. Since registry paths are absolute (per URD schema), the comparison must use `Path(entry.path).expanduser().resolve()` vs `cwd.resolve()`. This is implied but not stated in the SLICE-3 spec. If left to the implementer, a simple string comparison would silently fail for paths with symlinks or unexpanded tildes. Should be made explicit in the spec.

## Summary

All three BLOCKERs affect correctness of the production write path. The double-bump and rollback gaps are particularly dangerous as they can corrupt the user's marketplace.json in non-obvious ways.
title: PROPOSAL-1-REVIEW-A-1: Plugin registry + marketplace fix + test quality

## Reviewer B — Test Quality Axis — Round 2

**VOTE: ACCEPT**

### Round 1 BLOCKER Resolution Summary

All 4 Round 1 BLOCKERs are adequately addressed in PROPOSAL-2.

---

### B1 Resolution (Field-Level Round-Trip): ADEQUATE

AC3 now reads: "every entity matches field-by-field: ConstraintSpec.given/when/then/should_not, HandoffSpec.required_fields, SubstepSpec.startup_sequence, etc." The specific named fields (given/when/then/should_not, required_fields, startup_sequence) are concrete anchors that a worker cannot miss. The validation checklist reinforces with "Round-trip test: field-by-field comparison (not count-only)". The trailing "etc." is slightly loose but given the named fields cover the highest-risk data types, this is not a blocker.

Note: The existing test_schema_types_sync.py (line 294-308) already demonstrates the field-level pattern for CONSTRAINT_SPECS but omits should_not — AC3 correctly names should_not explicitly, which is an improvement over the current test coverage.

**BLOCKER RESOLVED.**

---

### B2 Resolution (Error-Path Tests): ADEQUATE

AC1a specifies three distinct failure inputs: malformed XML (truncated), missing required sections (e.g. missing `<phases>`), and missing required attributes. Requires "at least 3 error-path tests". The SchemaParseError class is defined in the module spec with a docstring: "Raised when schema.xml is malformed or missing required sections." Validation checklist entry: "Schema parser raises SchemaParseError on malformed input (3+ error-path tests)". This is specific and implementable.

**BLOCKER RESOLVED.**

---

### B3 Resolution (Marker Edge Cases): ADEQUATE

AC4a covers all three edge cases I requested:
1. Missing END marker → raises MarkerError
2. Missing both markers (new file) → generates full content (no error)
3. Reversed markers → raises MarkerError

MarkerError is defined as a ValueError subclass with docstring. The validation checklist has two separate entries: "gen_skills.py raises MarkerError on malformed markers" and "gen_skills.py handles new files (no markers) correctly". The "should not silently discard manual body on any boundary error" clause is the correct safety invariant.

**BLOCKER RESOLVED.**

---

### B4 Resolution (Absence Assertions): ADEQUATE

AC5a names concrete constraint IDs: C-worker-gates must NOT appear in get_role_context(SUPERVISOR). AC6a: C-severity-eager must NOT appear in get_phase_context(P4_REVIEW), C-review-consensus must NOT appear in get_phase_context(P9_SLICE). These are testable assertions against specific identifiers, not general "subset" language. Validation checklist: "get_role_context does NOT include irrelevant constraints" and "get_phase_context does NOT include irrelevant constraints".

**BLOCKER RESOLVED.**

---

### IMPORTANT Finding (Round 2 Observation): AC10 Pipeline Direction Ambiguity

AC10 describes the end-to-end test as: "parse_schema → populate types → gen_schema → validate_schema → gen_skills". This is the bootstrap direction (schema.xml → Python → schema.xml → SKILL.md). However, the steady-state pipeline (Python is SoT) runs in the opposite direction: types.py → gen_schema → validate_schema → gen_skills.

The validation checklist entry uses "parse → gen_schema → validate → gen_skills" which correctly omits the "populate types" step, suggesting the steady-state direction is intended. However AC10's description contradicts this.

This is not a BLOCKER because:
- Both pipeline directions are valid to test
- The validation checklist entry (the more implementation-facing document) uses the correct steady-state direction
- The confusion can be resolved by the worker reading both AC10 and the checklist together

**Suggestion for worker (not a blocker):** AC10 implementation should test BOTH directions — the bootstrap round-trip (schema.xml → SchemaSpec → gen_schema → validate → assert valid) AND the steady-state round-trip (types.py → gen_schema → parse_schema → assert field-level match). The latter is actually what AC3 already mandates; AC10 should be understood as the full pipeline integration test on top of AC3.

---

### Assessment of New ACs for Testability

| AC | Specific Enough? | Notes |
|----|-----------------|-------|
| AC1a | YES | 3 named failure inputs, 3+ tests required |
| AC3 | YES | Named fields per entity type; "not count-only" in checklist |
| AC4a | YES | 3 named cases, 2 raise, 1 generate-clean |
| AC5a | YES | Named constraint IDs (C-worker-gates, C-supervisor-no-impl) |
| AC6a | YES | Named constraint IDs (C-severity-eager, C-review-consensus) |
| AC8 | YES | Parametrized over all 4 role templates with StrictUndefined |
| AC10 | MOSTLY | Direction ambiguity noted above; not a blocker |

---

### Test Strategy Coverage Assessment

| Coverage Axis | Before R1 | After R2 |
|--------------|-----------|----------|
| Happy paths | Present | Present |
| Error paths (parser) | Missing | AC1a: 3+ negative tests |
| Error paths (generator) | Missing | AC4a: 2 raise, 1 clean |
| Field-level round-trip | Count-only | AC3: field-by-field per type |
| Absence assertions | Missing | AC5a/AC6a: named IDs |
| Parametrized templates | Single-role | AC8: all 4 roles |
| End-to-end pipeline | Missing | AC10: full chain |
| Sync test for new dicts | Not specified | AC9: 4 new dicts covered |

---

### Existing Test Infrastructure Compatibility

The conftest.py and test_schema_types_sync.py provide a strong foundation. The new tests should follow the same patterns:
- Module-scoped schema_root fixture (already in test_schema_types_sync.py) should be reused in test_gen_schema.py to avoid re-parsing schema.xml per test
- The field-level assertion pattern in test_constraint_given_when_then_match_schema (lines 294-308) is the correct model for AC3 implementation
- The tmp_path fixture from pytest should be used for AC4a marker edge case tests (already standard practice)

---

### Verdict

PROPOSAL-2 addresses all 4 Round 1 BLOCKERs with specific, implementable acceptance criteria. The test strategy is now comprehensive across error paths, boundary conditions, parametrized execution, absence assertions, and end-to-end pipeline coverage. The AC10 pipeline direction ambiguity is a minor implementation note, not a blocker. The validation checklist is complete and covers all test requirements.

**VOTE: ACCEPT**

## Specification

Extend gen_schema.py to emit new XML sections from types.py canonical dicts, and extend schema_parser.py to parse them back into typed Python objects. Full XML round-trip for all new entities.

### gen_schema.py Changes

#### New child elements under existing sections:
- <example> children under <constraint> with CDATA content (id, lang, label, also-illustrates attrs)
- <example> children under <step> (procedure steps) with CDATA content
- <introduction> child under <role> (text content)
- <ownership-narrative> child under <role> (text content)
- <behaviors>/<behavior> children under <role> (id, given, when, then, should-not attrs)
- command attribute on <constraint> elements (where applicable)

#### New top-level sections:
- <checklists>/<checklist> (role-ref, gate attrs) → <item> children (id, required, text)
- <coordination-commands>/<coord-cmd> (id, action, template, role-ref?, shared? attrs)
- <workflows>/<workflow> (id, name, role-ref, description) → <stage> children → <action> + <exit-condition> children

### schema_parser.py Changes

#### New parse functions:
- _parse_examples(parent_elem) → tuple[CodeExample, ...]
- _parse_behaviors(role_elem) → tuple[BehaviorSpec, ...]
- _parse_checklists(root) → dict[str, Checklist]
- _parse_coordination_commands(root) → dict[str, CoordinationCommand]
- _parse_workflows(root) → dict[str, Workflow]

#### SchemaSpec extensions:
- Add fields: checklists, coordination_commands, workflows
- Extend _parse_roles to extract introduction, ownership_narrative, behaviors
- Extend _parse_constraints to extract command, examples
- Extend _parse_procedure_steps to extract examples

### Key patterns from Cartographer A findings:
- gen_schema.py: _build_* functions take root ET.Element, read canonical dicts, emit via ET.SubElement
- schema_parser.py: _parse_* functions take root + path, extract attrs via _require(), return typed objects
- CDATA for code examples (DocBook pattern) — use xml.etree CDATA handling
- Follow existing function naming: _build_kebab_case() / _parse_kebab_case()

## Files Owned
- scripts/aura_protocol/gen_schema.py
- scripts/aura_protocol/schema_parser.py

## Acceptance Criteria
Given types.py with new canonical dicts when running gen_schema then schema.xml contains new sections (checklists, coordination-commands, workflows) and child elements (example, introduction, ownership-narrative, behaviors) should never break existing schema.xml structure

Given schema.xml with new elements when running schema_parser then SchemaSpec contains parsed checklists, coordination_commands, workflows, and extended roles/constraints/steps should never lose any existing parsed data

Given code examples in CDATA when parsing then raw code content preserved without XML escaping should never mangle code content

## Validation Checklist
- [ ] gen_schema.py emits <example> under <constraint> with CDATA
- [ ] gen_schema.py emits <example> under <step> with CDATA
- [ ] gen_schema.py emits <introduction>, <ownership-narrative>, <behaviors> under <role>
- [ ] gen_schema.py emits command attr on <constraint>
- [ ] gen_schema.py emits <checklists> top-level section
- [ ] gen_schema.py emits <coordination-commands> top-level section
- [ ] gen_schema.py emits <workflows> top-level section
- [ ] schema_parser.py parses all new child elements
- [ ] schema_parser.py parses all new top-level sections
- [ ] SchemaSpec extended with new fields
- [ ] Round-trip: gen → parse preserves all new data
- [ ] Existing schema structure unchanged
